ethical_notes: ETHICS: 'Lin-Yi' (林邑) is the Chinese exonym recorded in the Book of Han and Book of Jin for what became the northern Cham polities — the earliest attested independent state in central Vietnam after revolting from Han China in 192 CE under Khu Lien. The local name is uncertain; the polity is the direct proto-Cham phase preceding the Champa mandala of Indrapura-Vijaya-Panduranga. Vietnamese and Chinese sources dominate the record because no in-situ Cham epigraphy from this phase survives in usable quantity. The Sui conquest of 605 ended Lin-Yi as a distinct political label; successor polities reorganised as 'Huanwang' (環王) and 'Champa' proper. Keeping Lin-Yi as a distinct entity resists the Vietnamese nationalist flattening of Cham history into a single ethnically unified 'Champa 192-1832' narrative.

name_variants.name: Lâm Ấp
name_variants.context: Vietnamese reading of the Chinese name

name_variants.name: Rinyū
name_variants.context: Japanese reading used in East Asian scholarship

territory_changes.description: Khu Lien (Qu Lian / Sri Mara) led a revolt against Han commandery officials during the dying days of the Han dynasty, founding an independent polity around Xianglin district. The traditional date 192 CE rests on Chinese dynastic histories; the exact year is uncertain within a ±10-year window.

Sui general Liu Fang invaded and sacked the Lin-Yi capital, seizing an enormous war-booty of Buddhist texts, gold plates and musical instruments. Although the Sui withdrew, the Lin-Yi label disappears from Chinese records; Tang sources adopt the names 'Huanwang' and later 'Champa' (占城).

sources.citation: Maspero, G. Le Royaume de Champa. Paris: Van Oest, 1928
Vickery, M. 'Champa Revised', Asia Research Institute Working Paper Series No. 37, NUS, 2005
Book of Jin (晉書), chapter 97, 'Treatise on Southern Barbarians'

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Kahuripan is the short-lived reunification kingdom founded by Airlangga after the destruction of the Medang/Mataram court at Watan in 1016 by the Srivijayan-allied Wurawari raid. The name_original is Old Javanese; Indonesian historiography often subsumes Kahuripan into 'Mataram' or 'Medang', but the Pucangan inscription (1041) and the Calcutta Stone frame it as a distinct polity. At Airlangga's abdication in 1045 the kingdom was partitioned between his sons as Kadiri (Panjalu) and Janggala — so Kahuripan is the ancestor of the two mid-11th-century Javanese kingdoms and its separate existence matters for east-Javanese political genealogy.

name_variants.name: कहुरिपन
name_variants.context: Sanskritized form appearing in royal inscriptions

territory_changes.description: Airlangga, a refugee prince from the fallen Medang court, was consecrated king by surviving nobility and Brahmins after years in forest exile, reunifying fragments of the old Mataram realm.

Airlangga abdicated to become an ascetic and — to avoid a war of succession — had his advisor Mpu Bharada divide the kingdom between his sons Sri Samarawijaya (Panjalu/Kadiri) and Mapanji Garasakan (Janggala). The Pararaton and Calon Arang traditions describe this partition as a kingdom-ending ritual act.

sources.citation: Zoetmulder, P.J. Kalangwan: A Survey of Old Javanese Literature. The Hague: Nijhoff, 1974
Pucangan inscription (1041 CE), found at Mount Penanggungan; edited by Kern, BKI 1913
Munoz, P.M. Early Kingdoms of the Indonesian Archipelago and the Malay Peninsula. Editions Didier Millet, 2006

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Malayu kingdom (Mo-lo-yu in Tang sources, reported by the pilgrim Yijing in 671 CE) was absorbed by Srivijaya shortly after Yijing's visit, re-emerged as Bhumi Malayu / Dharmasraya in the late 13th century after Chola and Javanese raids weakened Srivijaya, and finally fused into Majapahit's Pamalayu sphere. Historiographically 'Malayu' is often erased by conflating it with Srivijaya (upstream of Srivijaya narratives) or with later Minangkabau. Keeping it distinct respects the indigenous Sumatran polity continuity between the 7th and 14th centuries and the Melayu ethnonym's origin.

name_variants.name: Mo-lo-yu
name_variants.context: Tang Chinese transcription in Yijing's account (671 CE)

name_variants.name: Dharmasraya
name_variants.context: Sanskrit name of the 13th-century upland successor capital

name_variants.name: Bhumi Malayu
name_variants.context: Sanskrit: 'Land of the Malayu', as referenced in the Padang Roco inscription (1286)

territory_changes.description: The Chinese Buddhist pilgrim Yijing records stopping at Mo-lo-yu on his journey from Srivijaya to India; he notes that on his return voyage Malayu had 'become Srivijaya', confirming its absorption between 671 and 685 CE.

The Padang Roco / Amoghapasa inscription records Kertanegara of Singhasari sending a Buddhist statue to Dharmasraya as diplomatic gift during the Pamalayu expedition — formalising Malayu's submission to Javanese overlordship.
Adityawarman, raised at the Majapahit court, became king and shifted the centre upstream to Pagaruyung, founding the Minangkabau royal tradition. The Malayu polity name fades from inscriptions.

sources.citation: Yijing (義淨), A Record of Buddhism as Practised in India. Translated by J. Takakusu. Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1896
Kulke, H. 'Srivijaya — a Great South-East Asian Empire or Kadatuan Srivijaya?' in Manguin, P-Y. (ed.) Early Interactions between South and Southeast Asia. Singapore: ISEAS, 2011
Padang Roco inscription (1286 CE); Amoghapasa inscription (1347 CE), edited by Kern and De Casparis

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Langkasuka is a Malay Peninsula Indianized kingdom mentioned in Chinese (Liang Shu, 狼牙脩), Tamil (Chola inscriptions) and Arab sources over more than a millennium. It is often conflated with its late-15th-century successor the Sultanate of Patani; our DB records Langkasuka merely as an alias there. Treating Langkasuka as a distinct polity (Hindu-Buddhist, then absorbed by the Islamic Patani transition c. 1457-1474) recovers pre-Islamic Malay political history frequently erased by post-conversion chronicles like Hikayat Patani.

name_variants.name: Ilangāsogam / Ilangāsokam
name_variants.context: Tamil form in the Larger Leiden Grant (1030) listing Chola targets

territory_changes.description: Liang Shu records a Langkasuka embassy to the Liang court; the kingdom is described as already several centuries old, suggesting a 1st–2nd-century founding.
Rajendra Chola I's naval raid listed Ilangāsogam among the plundered ports, inflicting damage Langkasuka never fully recovered from.
The polity transitioned into the Islamic Sultanate of Patani; the Langkasuka name disappears from Malay, Thai and Chinese records.

sources.citation: Wheatley, P. The Golden Khersonese: Studies in the Historical Geography of the Malay Peninsula before A.D. 1500. Kuala Lumpur: University of Malaya Press, 1961
Liang Shu (梁書), chapter 54, 'Account of the Southern Barbarians'
Nilakanta Sastri, K.A. Foreign Notices of South India: From Megasthenes to Ma Huan. Madras: University of Madras, 1939

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Kedah Tua ('Old Kedah') is the Hindu-Buddhist polity of the Bujang Valley, one of the oldest continuously inhabited archaeological complexes in maritime Southeast Asia (c. 110 temple sites, 5th–13th c. CE). It was vassalised by Srivijaya, then raided by Rajendra Chola (1025), before transitioning to an Islamic sultanate in 1136 under Sultan Muzaffar Shah I (born Phra Ong Mahawangsa). Malay chronicles (Hikayat Merong Mahawangsa) frame the transition as a straight 'conversion moment'; archaeology shows syncretic practice persisting for centuries. Treating Kedah Tua as distinct from the later sultanate prevents erasure of the pre-Islamic polity.

name_variants.name: Kataha
name_variants.context: Sanskrit form used in Kavya literature (Rajatarangini, Kathasaritsagara)

name_variants.name: Kalaha / Qalah
name_variants.context: Arab geographer references (Ibn Khordadbeh, 9th c.; Al-Masudi)

name_variants.name: Chieh-ch'a / 羯荼
name_variants.context: Tang Chinese transcription, recorded by Yijing (7th c.)

name_variants.name: Kaḍāram
name_variants.context: Tamil form in Chola inscriptions, notably the Tanjavur inscription (1030)

territory_changes.description: Earliest archaeological horizon at Sungai Mas with Indian ceramics and Buddhist finds; Kedah becomes a key transhipment point for cloves and camphor en route between India and China.

Rajendra Chola's South Indian fleet sacked Kadaram in the most devastating single event in the port's history; the Tamil inscription at Tanjavur lists 'Kadaram, whose waters are deep' among the conquered.
Conversion to Islam under Sultan Muzaffar Shah I; the polity becomes the Kedah Sultanate. Hindu-Buddhist structures at Lembah Bujang were progressively abandoned.

sources.citation: Jacq-Hergoualc'h, M. The Malay Peninsula: Crossroads of the Maritime Silk Road (100 BC-1300 AD). Leiden: Brill, 2002
Allen, S.J. 'Trade, Transportation, and Tributaries: Exchange, Agriculture, and Settlement Distribution in Early Historic-Period Kedah, Malaysia', PhD dissertation, University of Hawaii, 1988
Hikayat Merong Mahawangsa, Malay court chronicle, c. 1810 manuscript

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Sri Ksetra is the largest of the Pyu city-states in the central Irrawaddy basin, inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List in 2014. The Pyu were Tibeto-Burman speakers whose Buddhist civilization preceded the ethnic Burman arrival. Burmese nationalist historiography has sometimes absorbed the Pyu into a proto-Burmese narrative; archaeology and the Pyu-language inscriptions (a distinct language, now extinct) show a separate civilization that was conquered and culturally overwritten by the Nanzhao raids (832) and subsequent Bamar migration. Treating Sri Ksetra, Beikthano and Halin as distinct entities honours the pre-Burman Pyu heritage.

name_variants.name: 室利差呾羅
name_variants.context: Tang Chinese transcription in Xin Tang Shu

name_variants.name: Prome / Pyay
name_variants.context: modern English/Burmese city name of the ruin field

territory_changes.description: Earliest archaeological layers show urban occupation with Buddhist stupas; radiocarbon dates span the 1st century BCE onwards. The Vikrama dynasty tradition places founding even earlier.

The Nanzhao kingdom of Yunnan raided and destroyed Halin and severely damaged Sri Ksetra; Tang chronicles record 3,000 Pyu prisoners being deported to Yunnan. This initiated the Pyu collapse and the Bamar ethnogenesis around Pagan.

Sri Ksetra ceased to function as an urban political centre; the Pyu language and script survive only in isolated inscriptions thereafter. The Bamar polity at Pagan inherited the Theravada Buddhist and architectural legacy.

sources.citation: Aung-Thwin, M. The Mists of Ramañña: The Legend That Was Lower Burma. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 2005
Stargardt, J. The Ancient Pyu of Burma, Vol. 1: Early Pyu Cities in a Man-Made Landscape. Cambridge: PACSEA, 1990
UNESCO Pyu Ancient Cities nomination dossier (World Heritage List inscription 2014)

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Beikthano ('City of Vishnu') is the earliest Pyu walled city excavated, with occupation layers beginning in the late 1st millennium BCE — making it among the oldest urban sites in Southeast Asia. Like Sri Ksetra it belongs to the Pyu (Tibeto-Burman, not Bamar) cultural horizon. It is preserved on the UNESCO World Heritage inscription together with Sri Ksetra and Halin (2014).

name_variants.name: Vishnu City
name_variants.context: literal translation of the Burmese name

territory_changes.description: Earliest occupation layer at Beikthano; monumental brick architecture including palace platforms and Buddhist stupas appears by the 1st century CE.
Site abandoned in the wake of Pyu collapse and absorption into the emerging Bamar (Pagan) polity; no evidence of violent destruction at Beikthano itself, unlike Halin.

sources.citation: Aung Thaw. Report on the Excavations at Beikthano. Rangoon: Ministry of Union Culture, 1968
Hudson, B. 'The Origins of Bagan: The Archaeological Landscape of Upper Burma to AD 1300', PhD dissertation, University of Sydney, 2004
UNESCO Pyu Ancient Cities nomination dossier (2014)

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Halin is the northernmost of the major Pyu walled cities and the one explicitly recorded as destroyed by the Nanzhao raid of 832 CE (Xin Tang Shu). Part of the 2014 UNESCO World Heritage inscription 'Pyu Ancient Cities' together with Sri Ksetra and Beikthano. Its destruction catalysed the Pyu-to-Bamar ethnogenesis.

name_variants.name: Mi-ch'en / 彌臣
name_variants.context: Chinese transcription in the Man Shu (9th c.)

territory_changes.description: Walled urban phase begins; radiocarbon dates for the earliest brick structures cluster in the 2nd–4th century CE.

Nanzhao king Quan Fengyou sacked Halin, deported 3,000 residents as labour to the Yunnan court, and installed a Nanzhao garrison at the site. This is recorded in the Xin Tang Shu (New Book of Tang) and in the Man Shu ethnographic treatise.

sources.citation: Man Shu (蠻書 'Book of the Southern Barbarians') by Fan Chuo, c. 863 CE
Hudson, B. 'The Origins of Bagan', PhD dissertation, University of Sydney, 2004
Aung-Thwin, M. The Mists of Ramañña. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 2005

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Thaton (Sudhammavati) is the semi-legendary Mon Theravada Buddhist kingdom of Lower Burma destroyed by Anawrahta of Pagan in 1057 CE, an event that supposedly transferred the Pali Tipitaka, Mon architectural traditions and thousands of Mon monks, artisans and captives to Pagan. Michael Aung-Thwin's The Mists of Ramañña (2005) argues that much of the received Mon-Thaton narrative is a later Burmese-Mon literary elaboration, but archaeology confirms a Mon polity of some sort. Confidence is explicitly low because of this active historiographic dispute — honesty about uncertainty is more valuable than a fake-precise founding date. Anawrahta's 1057 conquest is, however, well-attested in Burmese, Mon and epigraphic sources.

name_variants.name: Sudhammavati
name_variants.context: Pali name in Mon and Sinhalese Buddhist chronicles

name_variants.name: Suwannabhumi (disputed identification)
name_variants.context: Pali: 'Land of Gold'; Burmese/Mon tradition identifies Thaton with the Ashokan missionary destination of Suvarnabhumi, a claim contested by historians

territory_changes.description: Approximate founding per traditional Mon chronicles; the exact date is highly uncertain. Archaeological evidence for large-scale Mon urbanism at Thaton is harder to pin down than at contemporary Dvaravati sites in Thailand.

Anawrahta of Pagan captured Thaton, extracted the Pali Tipitaka, and deported the Mon king Manuha plus a large portion of the Mon population — monks, artisans, scholars — northward to Pagan. This event is the mythic foundation of the Burmese Theravada tradition; the scale of deportation amounts to a demographic depopulation of Thaton.

sources.citation: Aung-Thwin, M. The Mists of Ramañña: The Legend That Was Lower Burma. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 2005
Luce, G.H. Old Burma — Early Pagán, 3 vols. Locust Valley: Artibus Asiae, 1969-70
Hmannan Yazawin (Glass Palace Chronicle), Burmese royal chronicle, 1832

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Dhanyawadi is the first of three successive Arakanese (Rakhine) capitals (Dhanyawadi → Waithali → Lemro). Traditional Rakhine chronicles (Rakhine Razawin Thit) push the founding as early as the 6th century BCE; archaeology more cautiously places the walled city in the early centuries CE. The Mahamuni Buddha image, one of the most venerated Buddha images in Theravada Buddhism, is traditionally attributed to Dhanyawadi. Rakhine historiography is contested: the 2017 genocide against the Rohingya has made historical questions of Arakan identity politically charged, and some Rakhine chronicles have been mobilised to frame Rohingya Muslims as recent outsiders. A careful entity record that cites the archaeological uncertainty is a small contribution against that weaponisation.

territory_changes.description: Traditional founding date per the Rakhine royal chronicle Rakhine Razawin Thit. Archaeological evidence for urban occupation at the site begins far later (1st–4th c. CE); confidence is therefore very low for the early chronology.
The political centre shifted to the new capital Waithali (Vesali), beginning the second Arakanese dynasty. Dhanyawadi was not violently destroyed; it was simply eclipsed.

sources.citation: Gutman, P. Ancient Arakan: With Special Reference to its Cultural History between the Fifth and Eleventh Centuries. PhD dissertation, Australian National University, 1976

Charney, M.W. 'Arakan, Min Yazagyi, and the Portuguese: The Relationship between the Growth of Arakanese Imperial Power and Portuguese Mercenaries on the Fringe of Mainland Southeast Asia 1517-1617', MA thesis, Ohio University, 1993
Rakhine Razawin Thit (New Rakhine Chronicle), 18th–19th c. Rakhine royal chronicle

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Vesali (Waithali) is the second of the three ancient Arakanese capitals, documented by substantial epigraphy (Anandacandra inscription, c. 729 CE) and archaeology. The Arakan region's Candra dynasty maintained a mixed Hindu-Mahayana Buddhist court with ties to Bengal and Magadha; the region was then culturally closer to the Ganges basin than to the Irrawaddy. The 8th-century Arakanese Candra dynasty is the second of two dynasties of that name (the first being the Chandras of Vikrampur, Bengal), and the two are not infrequently conflated in English-language sources.

name_variants.name: Vaiśālī
name_variants.context: Sanskrit form; sharing the name of the Licchavi capital in North India is intentional

territory_changes.description: Approximate founding per Arakanese chronicles. The first Candra king recorded in the Anandacandra inscription (c. 729) is Dvan Candra, placed chronologically at the start of the 4th century.

Rakhine chronicles record the shift of capital to the Lemro valley, initiating the Lemro period. The transition is linked to dynastic change and possibly to Pyu or Nanzhao-related regional disruption.

sources.citation: Anandacandra Inscription, Shitthaung Pillar, c. 729 CE; edited by E.H. Johnston, BSOAS 11 (1944)
Gutman, P. Burma's Lost Kingdoms: Splendours of Arakan. Bangkok: Orchid Press, 2001
Leider, J.P. 'Arakan, a Remote Periphery in the History of Wars in Pre-modern Southeast Asia', Journal of Burma Studies, 2018

capital_name: Sambawak / Pyinsa / Parin / Hkrit / Launggret / Toungoo (successive)
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Lemro period is a sequence of at least six Rakhine court cities in the Lemro river valley between the fall of Vesali (818) and the founding of Mrauk-U (1430). It is poorly documented compared to the bracketing periods and may represent a single dynasty with shifting capitals, a series of competing local dynasties, or a hybrid. Treating it as a single entity with low confidence and multiple capital names is the honest compromise until archaeology clarifies. Burmese (Pagan), Mon (Pegu) and Bengali (Pala → Delhi Sultanate) powers all raided or claimed overlordship during this period.

name_variants.name: လေးမြို့ခေတ်
name_variants.context: Burmese: 'four-city period', though up to six capitals are traditionally counted

territory_changes.description: Political centre shifted from Vesali inland to the Lemro valley, inaugurating a polycentric court sequence whose dynastic list varies across chronicles.
Anawrahta of Pagan reportedly raided Arakan and briefly installed tribute-paying rulers; Arakan oscillated between autonomy and Pagan/Bengal overlordship for the remainder of the period.
King Min Saw Mon founded the Kingdom of Mrauk-U with Bengali (Gaur Sultanate) military support after two decades of exile, opening the classical period of Arakanese imperial expansion.

sources.citation: Gutman, P. Burma's Lost Kingdoms: Splendours of Arakan. Bangkok: Orchid Press, 2001

Charney, M.W. 'Where Jambudipa and Islamdom Converged: Religious Change and the Emergence of Buddhist Communalism in Early Modern Arakan (Fifteenth to Nineteenth Centuries)', PhD dissertation, University of Michigan, 1999

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Hariphunchai was a Mon Theravada Buddhist kingdom in the Ping river valley of what is now northern Thailand — the northernmost Dvaravati-cultural outlier. Founded, per Cāmadevīvaṃsa, by Queen Cāmadevī c. 629 CE sent from Lavo, it preserved Mon culture in the north until conquered by King Mangrai of Lan Na in 1292. Mangrai deliberately preserved Mon monks, artisans and Buddhist institutions, making Hariphunchai's legacy foundational to Lan Na civilization. The kingdom is often absent from English-language Southeast Asia surveys despite its importance.

name_variants.name: Haripunjaya
name_variants.context: alternative romanization common in older literature

territory_changes.description: Per the Cāmadevīvaṃsa, the ascetic Vasudeva invited Princess Cāmadevī from the Mon-Khmer kingdom of Lavo to found a new capital at Lamphun; she ruled as queen and established the Theravada monastic order. The date is traditional and contested — archaeology suggests the city may be slightly later (8th century).

King Mangrai of Ngoenyang conquered Hariphunchai after a long diplomatic penetration (a spy-minister, Ai Fa, had been planted in the Hariphunchai court for seven years). Mangrai made the captured capital the nucleus of Lan Na; Mon Theravada institutions were preserved and absorbed.

sources.citation: Coedès, G. The Indianized States of Southeast Asia. Trans. S.B. Cowing. Honolulu: East-West Center Press, 1968
Swearer, D.K. & Sommai Premchit. The Legend of Queen Cama: Bodhiramsi's Camadevivamsa, a Translation and Commentary. Albany: SUNY Press, 1998
Cāmadevīvaṃsa, Pali chronicle by Mahāthera Bodhiraṃsi, c. 1410 CE

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Ngô dynasty is the first independent Vietnamese dynasty after almost a thousand years of Chinese rule (the 'Bắc thuộc' / 'Chinese domination' periods, 111 BCE–939 CE). Founded by Ngô Quyền after his decisive 938 naval victory at the Bạch Đằng River against the Southern Han, it is absent from AtlasPI despite being the foundation event of Vietnamese independence. The dynasty collapsed into the 'Twelve Warlords Period' (Thập Nhị Sứ Quân) before Đinh Bộ Lĩnh reunified it in 968.

name_variants.name: Tĩnh Hải quân (Tĩnh Hải Army)
name_variants.context: formal name used by Ngô Quyền, inherited from Tang administrative titles

territory_changes.description: Ngô Quyền proclaimed himself king at Cổ Loa after defeating the Southern Han fleet at Bạch Đằng River (late 938) using iron-tipped stakes planted in the river bed — an event revered as the founding moment of Vietnamese statehood.
Ngô Quyền died; his brother-in-law Dương Tam Kha usurped the throne, triggering the fragmentation that became the Twelve Warlords Period.
Đinh Bộ Lĩnh defeated the last Ngô claimants and the remaining warlords, ending the Ngô dynasty and unifying the territory as Đại Cồ Việt.

sources.citation: Taylor, K.W. The Birth of Vietnam. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1983
Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư (Complete Annals of Đại Việt), compiled by Ngô Sĩ Liên, 1479
Whitmore, J.K. 'Ngô (Chinese: Wu) Dynasty', in Encyclopedia of Asian History, Scribner's, 1988

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Đinh dynasty ended the Twelve Warlords Period and gave Vietnam its first formal imperial name — Đại Cồ Việt ('Great Viet') — with Đinh Bộ Lĩnh taking the title Hoàng Đế ('emperor'), an unprecedented challenge to Chinese claims of sole imperial authority in the region. The dynasty also established the diplomatic template for Vietnamese-Chinese relations: Song China recognised Đinh as 'King of Jiaozhi', a nominal vassalage concealing substantive independence. Assassinations within the court ended the dynasty in 980.

name_variants.name: Đại Cồ Việt
name_variants.context: formal state name during and after the Đinh

territory_changes.region: Red River delta and northern Annam
territory_changes.description: Đinh Bộ Lĩnh, son of a minor chieftain from Hoa Lư, suppressed the Twelve Warlords, adopted the title Hoàng Đế, and named the state Đại Cồ Việt. His new capital at Hoa Lư was chosen for defensibility in the karst landscape of Ninh Bình rather than the Red River delta.
Đinh Bộ Lĩnh and his heir Đinh Liễn were assassinated by the palace eunuch Đỗ Thích, leaving a minor on the throne.

The general Lê Hoàn, acting regent, was elevated to the throne by the court in response to an imminent Song Chinese invasion. The Đinh dynasty effectively ended; the new ruler founded the Tiền Lê (Early Lê) dynasty.

sources.citation: Taylor, K.W. A History of the Vietnamese. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2013
Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư (Complete Annals of Đại Việt), Ngô Sĩ Liên, 1479

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Early Lê (Tiền Lê) dynasty — distinct from the later Lê dynasty of 1428-1789 already in the database as 'Nhà Lê' — is an absent link between Đinh and Lý. Founder Lê Đại Hành (Lê Hoàn) repelled the 981 Song invasion at Bạch Đằng (a second Vietnamese naval victory on the same river in 43 years), and launched the first recorded Vietnamese attack southward into Champa, sacking Indrapura in 982. The dynasty ended when Lý Công Uẩn deposed the dissolute Lê Long Đĩnh in 1009.

name_variants.name: Early Lê dynasty
name_variants.context: standard English designation, distinguishing from the Hậu Lê (Later Lê) 1428-1789

territory_changes.description: Lê Hoàn took the throne from the Đinh minor Đinh Toàn; the widow regent Dương Vân Nga publicly draped him in the imperial robe in a ceremony recorded in the Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư. Some Confucian Vietnamese historians later condemned this as irregular.

Lê Hoàn led the first major Vietnamese southward military campaign, sacking the Cham capital Indrapura (near modern Đà Nẵng) and killing King Parameshvaravarman I. This was the opening act of the centuries-long Nam Tiến ('southern advance') that would destroy Champa in stages.

After the dissolute reign of Lê Long Đĩnh (nicknamed Lê Ngọa Triều, 'the king who ruled lying down', due to illness), the palace guard commander Lý Công Uẩn was elevated by the court, founding the Lý dynasty.

sources.citation: Taylor, K.W. A History of the Vietnamese. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2013
Whitmore, J.K. 'Elephants Can Actually Swim: Contemporary Chinese Views of Late Ly Dai Viet', in Marr & Milner (eds.) Southeast Asia in the 9th to 14th Centuries. Singapore: ISEAS, 1986
Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư, Ngô Sĩ Liên, 1479

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Hồ dynasty is a short, consequential and extraordinarily interventionist Vietnamese polity founded when Hồ Quý Ly — a Sino-Vietnamese regent — deposed the last Trần emperor and renamed the state Đại Ngu. Hồ Quý Ly attempted radical reforms (land redistribution, paper currency, mass military conscription, Neo-Confucian examinations in Nôm) that prefigured modern state-building. The Ming Yongle Emperor used Trần restorationist pretexts to invade in 1406, captured the Hồ emperors, and annexed Vietnam as the Jiaozhi province (1407-1427) — an occupation that inflicted enormous cultural damage including the systematic burning of Vietnamese books. The UNESCO World Heritage Citadel of the Hồ Dynasty (inscribed 2011) survives as a monumental witness.

name_variants.name: Đại Ngu
name_variants.context: the state name adopted by Hồ Quý Ly (大虞); 'Ngu' meaning 'peace' / refers to legendary Emperor Shun (虞舜)

territory_changes.description: Hồ Quý Ly deposed the young Trần emperor Thiếu Đế, assumed the throne, renamed the state Đại Ngu and moved the capital south to the newly built Tây Đô (Western Capital) at Thành Nhà Hồ in Thanh Hóa.

The Ming Yongle Emperor launched a 215,000-strong invasion under pretext of restoring the Trần dynasty. The Hồ garrisons collapsed in months; Hồ Quý Ly and his son Hồ Hán Thương were captured and taken to Nanjing.

Ming China annexed Đại Ngu as Jiaozhi (交趾) province. The 20-year occupation imposed Chinese-style administration, seized or burned a large fraction of the extant Vietnamese literary heritage, and forcibly Sinicized clothing and rites. Resistance under Lê Lợi (1418) culminated in independence in 1427.

sources.citation: Whitmore, J.K. Vietnam, Ho Quy Ly, and the Ming (1371-1421). New Haven: Yale Southeast Asia Studies, 1985
Taylor, K.W. A History of the Vietnamese. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2013
UNESCO Citadel of the Ho Dynasty, World Heritage List inscription 2011

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Ma-i (麻逸) is a pre-colonial Philippine polity attested from 971 CE in the Song Shi and Zhu Fan Zhi (1225) trade records, the earliest documented polity in the northern Philippine archipelago. Its exact location is contested: scholarly consensus oscillates between Mindoro, the shores of Laguna de Bay (home of the 900 CE Laguna Copperplate), and Bay in northern Palawan. Low confidence reflects this ambiguity, not the existence of the polity. Pre-Hispanic Philippine history is chronically underrepresented, partly because Spanish colonial historiography treated pre-1565 indigenous states as stateless societies — a framing contemporary archaeology has refuted.

territory_changes.description: Song dynasty records document Ma-i merchants trading porcelain and textiles at Canton — the first externally documented Philippine trade mission.
Chinese author Zhao Rugua's Zhu Fan Zhi describes Ma-i as a group of large islands with honest traders, barkcloth-wearing elites, and an intricate silent-barter system with Chinese merchants.

The Yuan-era Daoyi Zhilüe by Wang Dayuan is the last external source to mention Ma-i by that name; subsequent Ming records speak of Luzon (呂宋), reflecting either political change or terminological drift.

sources.citation: Scott, W.H. Prehispanic Source Materials for the Study of Philippine History, rev. ed. Quezon City: New Day Publishers, 1984
Zhao Rugua (趙汝适), Zhu Fan Zhi (諸蕃志, 'Records of Foreign Peoples'), 1225; trans. F. Hirth and W.W. Rockhill, St Petersburg, 1911
Wang Dayuan (汪大淵), Daoyi Zhilüe (島夷誌略 'A Brief Account of Island Barbarians'), 1349

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Namayan, also called Sapa or Maysapan, is the pre-colonial polity of the Pasig river's south bank, contemporary with and partially rival to Tondo and Maynila. Excavations at Santa Ana (1966-1968 by Alfredo Evangelista and Robert Fox) produced a stratified 12th-14th c. burial layer with Song and Yuan trade ceramics, establishing that Namayan was a significant polity decades before Tondo reaches external records (the Laguna Copperplate places Tondo in 900 CE, but dense archaeological evidence for Namayan begins around the 12th century). Spanish colonisation (1571) absorbed Namayan into the encomienda system; by the 18th century even local residents struggled to recall the polity's name.

territory_changes.description: Approximate date of archaeologically attested urban occupation at Santa Ana; lowest burial strata are datable to the 12th century by associated Song trade ceramics.

Miguel López de Legazpi's forces absorbed Namayan (alongside Tondo and Maynila) into the Spanish encomienda system after the defeat of Rajah Soliman's coalition. Native leaders such as the dato Lakan Tagkan retained symbolic offices briefly before full colonial incorporation.

sources.citation: Fox, R.B. 'The Archaeological Record of Chinese Influences in the Philippines', Philippine Studies 15.1 (1967), pp. 41-62
Scott, W.H. Barangay: Sixteenth-Century Philippine Culture and Society. Quezon City: Ateneo de Manila University Press, 1994

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Caboloan (also called Luyag na Kaboloan, or Pangasinan) is the Pangasinan polity of Luzon's Lingayen Gulf, attested in Ming dynastic histories (明史) as 'Feng-chia-hsi-lan' / 馮嘉施蘭. Ming Yongle emperor recognised a ruler named Kamayin with a diplomatic mission in 1406-1411; a second mission in 1417 under Taymey is also recorded. Caboloan was absorbed into Spanish Pangasinan after 1571 conquests and the 1660-1661 Malong Revolt. Spanish colonial and later American colonial historiography elided Caboloan into 'pre-Hispanic Filipino barangays'; recognising it as a polity with external diplomatic relations reclaims indigenous statehood.

name_variants.name: Pangasinan
name_variants.context: modern name derived from pangasinan — 'place where salt is made'

territory_changes.description: First Ming diplomatic mission from Caboloan under ruler Kamayin arrived at the court of the Yongle Emperor, recorded in the Ming Shilu (明實錄).
Juan de Salcedo's Spanish expedition subjugated the Pangasinan elite; the polity was absorbed into the Spanish encomienda system, though substantial Pangasinan autonomy persisted until the 1660s.

sources.citation: Scott, W.H. Looking for the Prehispanic Filipino. Quezon City: New Day Publishers, 1992
Ming Shilu (明實錄 'Veritable Records of the Ming'), Yongle reign entries 1406-1417
Cortes, R.M. Pangasinan, 1572-1800. Quezon City: University of the Philippines Press, 1974

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The 'Confederation of Madja-as' is the polity described in the Maragtas, a 1907 narrative by Pedro Alcantara Monteclaro drawing on oral tradition about ten Bornean datus (led by Datu Puti) who fled the Brunei overlord Makatunaw c. 1212 CE and settled Panay, purchasing the island from the Ati chief Marikudo. Historians including William Henry Scott (1968) have shown the Maragtas narrative to be a 20th-century literary composition rather than a transparent chronicle — yet some Panay barangays preserve oral variants that appear genuinely old. Confidence is deliberately very low and status is 'disputed'. Including Madja-as with explicit uncertainty honours both (a) the political reality of a pre-Hispanic Panay polity structure, and (b) the scholarly critique of the canonical Maragtas chronology.

name_variants.name: Confederation of Madja-as
name_variants.context: English-language term popularised in mid-20th-c. Philippine textbooks

name_variants.name: Aninipay
name_variants.context: indigenous name for Panay island attested in the Maragtas narrative

territory_changes.description: Traditional date from the Maragtas for the Bornean-datu settlement on Panay; academic historians reject this as literary reconstruction. Archaeologically, sophisticated Panay settlement pre-dates 1212, so a pre-Hispanic polity existed but the founding story is not reliable history.

Miguel López de Legazpi's Spanish force, after leaving Cebu, established a base on Panay from which subsequent Visayan colonisation was launched. Local polities lost political autonomy; the Maragtas tradition was composed much later against this loss.

sources.citation: Scott, W.H. 'The Maragtas', in A Critical Study of the Prehispanic Source Materials for the Study of Philippine History, pp. 91-103. Quezon City: University of Santo Tomas Press, 1968
Monteclaro, P.A. Maragtas. Iloilo, 1907

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Samudera Pasai is the first Islamic sultanate of maritime Southeast Asia and arguably the mother polity of 'Sumatra' as a toponym (Marco Polo's 'Samara' derives from it). Its founder Sultan Malik al-Saleh converted c. 1267; Ibn Battuta visited in 1345-46 and described the court as a centre of Shafi'i learning. Pasai mediated the Islamisation of the Malay world before Melaka took over that role. The Aceh Sultanate absorbed Pasai in 1521; later Acehnese historiography subsumed Pasai's independent identity. Recognising Pasai separately restores an under-told chapter in the Islamic history of Southeast Asia.

name_variants.name: Sumatra / Samudra
name_variants.context: the island name Sumatra derives from this polity

territory_changes.description: Sultan Malik al-Saleh (before conversion: Merah Silu) converted to Islam and founded the sultanate; his tombstone (d. 1297) is the oldest surviving Islamic royal grave marker in Southeast Asia.

Ibn Battuta visited Sultan al-Malik al-Zahir and recorded Pasai as a sincere Shafi'i sultanate that promoted Islamic jurisprudence and war against non-Muslim neighbours. This is one of the richest external ethnographic accounts of early Muslim Southeast Asia.
The Aceh Sultanate under Ali Mughayat Syah conquered Pasai; the port was annexed, though Portuguese forces had sacked it in 1521 first. Pasai never recovered as an independent entity.

sources.citation: Hill, A.H. (trans.) 'Hikayat Raja-Raja Pasai', Journal of the Malayan Branch of the Royal Asiatic Society 33.2 (1960), pp. 1-215
Reid, A. An Indonesian Frontier: Acehnese and Other Histories of Sumatra. Singapore: NUS Press, 2005
Ibn Battuta, Riḥla (Tuḥfat an-Nuẓẓār fī Gharāʾib al-Amṣār wa ʿAjāʾib al-Asfār), c. 1355

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Peureulak (Perlak) is claimed by Acehnese tradition as the oldest Islamic sultanate in Southeast Asia, founded 840 CE, two and a half centuries before Pasai. The evidence rests largely on the 1970s seminary by Ali Hasjmy ('Sejarah Masuk dan Berkembangnya Islam di Indonesia') which has been heavily critiqued for over-reading late chronicle fragments. Marco Polo did visit 'Ferlec' in 1292 and described it as the first Muslim-ruled town he encountered in the archipelago, which confirms a 13th-c. sultanate but does not verify the 9th-c. founding. Low confidence reflects the historiographic dispute.

name_variants.name: Ferlec
name_variants.context: Marco Polo's transcription in Il Milione (1298)

territory_changes.description: Traditional Acehnese dating for the founding of the sultanate; this date is accepted by some Indonesian textbooks but contested by academic historians.
Marco Polo stopped at Ferlec en route home from China and wrote that its ruler and population had converted to Islam through Arab merchant contact — the earliest credible external attestation.
Peureulak was absorbed by the neighbouring Samudera Pasai Sultanate in the same late 13th century; the two became one polity.

sources.citation: Marco Polo, Il Milione (The Travels of Marco Polo), c. 1298
Hasjmy, A. Sejarah Masuk dan Berkembangnya Islam di Indonesia. Bandung: Al-Ma'arif, 1983 (disputed)
Reid, A. An Indonesian Frontier: Acehnese and Other Histories of Sumatra. Singapore: NUS Press, 2005

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Haru (Aru) controlled the eastern coast of North Sumatra around the Karo Batak highlands. It is attested in Chinese sources (Zhu Fan Zhi 1225 as 'He-lu'), the Nagarakretagama (1365) as a vassal of Majapahit, and the Malay Hikayat Raja-Raja Pasai as a rival and intermittent enemy of Pasai. The polity was Karo Batak in ethnic base, progressively Islamicised, and finally destroyed by Aceh under Iskandar Muda in 1613. The Kota Cina archaeological site (9th-14th c. trade emporium) attests to its commercial vitality; Batak identity, often under-represented in a Malay-centric Sumatran historiography, deserves explicit recognition.

name_variants.name: He-lu / 訶陵
name_variants.context: Song and Ming Chinese transcription — note: 訶陵 is more commonly associated with Kalingga in central Java; Haru uses different characters in some Ming sources

name_variants.name: Aru Barumun
name_variants.context: full name reflecting the Barumun river region

territory_changes.description: Zhao Rugua's Zhu Fan Zhi documents He-lu among Sumatran ports trading in camphor, rattan and benzoin.
Mpu Prapanca's Nagarakretagama lists Haru among the nusantara vassals of Majapahit, though the substance of this 'suzerainty' is debated.
Sultan Iskandar Muda of Aceh destroyed Haru in a major campaign, deporting its elite to Aceh proper. The remaining Karo Batak population retreated inland; the coastal polity ceased to exist.

sources.citation: McKinnon, E.E. 'Kota Cina: Its Context in the Trade of Southeast Asia in the Twelfth to Fourteenth Centuries', PhD dissertation, Cornell University, 1984
Miksic, J.N. Singapore and the Silk Road of the Sea, 1300-1800. Singapore: NUS Press, 2013
Mpu Prapanca, Nagarakretagama (Deśawarnana), 1365 CE

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Ngoenyang ('Silver Yang') is the Tai-Yuan kingdom of the upper Mekong region from which Mangrai launched the conquest of Hariphunchai in 1292 and founded Lan Na. Traditional chronicles (Jinakalamali, Chiang Mai Chronicle) give founding dates as early as 638 CE under King Lavachangkarat — this is legendary rather than historically verifiable. Ngoenyang represents the proto-Lan Na Tai polity and links the historical Tai migration into northern Southeast Asia.

territory_changes.description: Traditional founding date per the Jinakalamali. Archaeology at Chiang Saen suggests urban occupation begins several centuries later than the chronicle date.
King Mangrai, 25th ruler of Ngoenyang, founded Chiang Rai as a new capital, beginning territorial expansion.
Mangrai conquered Hariphunchai after seven years of espionage by his agent Ai Fa; Ngoenyang was transformed into Lan Na with the eventual capital at Chiang Mai (founded 1296).

sources.citation: Wyatt, D.K. Thailand: A Short History, 2nd ed. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2003
Jinakalamali, Pali chronicle by Ratanapañña, 1516 CE; trans. N.A. Jayawickrama, Pali Text Society, 1968
Chiang Mai Chronicle, trans. D.K. Wyatt & Aroonrut Wichienkeeo. Chiang Mai: Silkworm Books, 1995

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Phayao was an independent Tai mueang east of Ngoenyang/Lan Na, founded by Chom Tham c. 1094 CE. Ally of Ngoenyang under King Ngam Muang (r. c. 1258-1298) — a friend of Mangrai and Ramkhamhaeng of Sukhothai; the three kings supposedly made a pact under a mythic meeting at Kwan Phayao lake. Phayao retained distinct identity until conquest by Lan Na in 1338. It is often absent from general Thai histories despite producing important Pali Buddhist scholarship.

territory_changes.description: Founding under Chom Tham per the Phayao Chronicle; a distinct Tai-Yuan mueang east of Ngoenyang.
King Ngam Muang, Mangrai and Ramkhamhaeng reportedly concluded a three-way pact of non-aggression that shaped northern Thai geopolitics for a generation.
Lan Na under King Kham Fu annexed Phayao, ending its independent existence.

sources.citation: Penth, H. A Brief History of Lan Na: Northern Thailand from Past to Present. Chiang Mai: Silkworm Books, 2004
Tamnan Mueang Phayao (Phayao Chronicle), late Lan Na period palm-leaf manuscript

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Bedulu (sometimes 'Pejeng kingdom' or Warmadewa dynasty) is the pre-Majapahit Balinese polity attested by the 914 CE Blanjong Pillar at Sanur (bilingual Sanskrit-Old Balinese), in which King Sri Kesari Warmadewa proclaims victory. Bali is often narrated as a 'Hindu-Majapahit refugee society' after the 1343 Gajah Mada conquest, but the Warmadewa Blanjong evidence shows three centuries of prior independent Balinese Hindu statehood. The 1343 Majapahit conquest killed the last king Bedulu (or his general Pasung Grigis in variants); Bedulu became a Majapahit vassal and later the Gelgel court (already in DB) succeeded it.

name_variants.name: Warmadewa dynasty
name_variants.context: academic designation for the ruling house 914-1343

name_variants.name: Pejeng kingdom
name_variants.context: alternative based on the archaeological site of Pejeng near Bedulu

territory_changes.description: The Blanjong Pillar at Sanur records King Sri Kesari Warmadewa's military victories and dedicates a victory monument — the earliest dated attestation of Balinese statehood.

Majapahit forces under Gajah Mada conquered Bali; the last Bedulu king was defeated. Local tradition says the king was supernaturally cursed (the 'Bedulu' legend involving a head exchange); historians see the legend as a narrative softening of a conquest.

sources.citation: Ardika, I.W. 'Bali and the Nusantara Region Throughout Prehistoric and Historical Times', in Reuter, T. (ed.) Inequality, Crisis and Social Change in Indonesia. London: RoutledgeCurzon, 2003
Blanjong Pillar inscription, Sanur, 914 CE
Hinzler, H.I.R. 'The Usana Bali as a Source of History', in Vatter, E. (ed.) Studien zur Indonesischen Geschichte und Kultur. Stuttgart: Steiner, 1986

ethical_notes: ETHICS: An earlier Balinese polity attested by the Sukawana A I inscription (882 CE) predating Warmadewa Bedulu. The inscription — one of the earliest Old Balinese-language documents — records royal grants to Buddhist monastic communities (wihara) near Mount Batur. Bali's Buddhist layer before the Hindu Warmadewa consolidation is rarely told; this entity exists specifically to resist that erasure. Confidence deliberately low because only a handful of inscriptions attest this phase and the polity name is a scholarly convention, not an indigenous label.

name_variants.name: Sukawana polity
name_variants.context: alternative academic designation based on the findspot of the earliest inscription

name_variants.name: Old Balinese period
name_variants.context: umbrella term covering all pre-Warmadewa polities

territory_changes.description: The Sukawana A I copper-plate inscription records royal endowments to Buddhist monastic institutions, the earliest dated Old Balinese legal document.
The Warmadewa dynasty of Bedulu succeeded this polity; the transition is unclear and may have been dynastic continuity rather than replacement.

sources.citation: Goris, R. Prasasti Bali, 2 vols. Bandung: Lembaga Bahasa dan Budaja, 1954
Sukawana A I inscription, 882 CE; edited in Goris, Prasasti Bali Vol. I

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Indrapura is the phase of Champa centered on the northern capital Đồng Dương (875-982), also called the 'Fourth Dynasty' of Champa. Distinguished by Indravarman II's establishment of the great Đồng Dương Mahayana Buddhist monastery (875 CE) — a rare Mahayana phase in otherwise Shaiva Cham history. Destroyed by Lê Hoàn's Đại Cồ Việt in 982, after which the capital retreated south to Vijaya. Treating Indrapura as a distinct phase resists the 'single Champa' flattening in the existing DB record.

name_variants.name: Đồng Dương
name_variants.context: modern Vietnamese name of the archaeological site

territory_changes.description: King Indravarman II proclaimed himself founder of a new capital at Indrapura and built the Đồng Dương Mahayana Buddhist monastery, an extraordinary shift from the prevailing Shaivism.

Lê Hoàn's Early Lê expedition sacked Indrapura, killed King Parameshvaravarman I, and seized treasures including Cham court musicians (recorded separately in the Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư). The Cham political centre retreated to Vijaya, ending the Indrapura phase.

sources.citation: Maspero, G. Le Royaume de Champa. Paris: Van Oest, 1928
Hubert, J.-F. The Art of Champa. New York: Parkstone Press, 2005
Southworth, W.A. 'The Coastal States of Champa', in Bellwood, P. & Glover, I. (eds.) Southeast Asia: From Prehistory to History. London: Routledge, 2004

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Vijaya Champa is the long classical phase of the Cham polity after the forced retreat from Indrapura in 982. The dynasty faced repeated Khmer invasions (notably Suryavarman II in 1145-1149, conquering Vijaya briefly) and Vietnamese advances. The 1471 Vietnamese campaign under Lê Thánh Tông destroyed Vijaya and reduced Champa to a rump at Panduranga; Vietnamese sources record 60,000 Cham killed and 30,000 captured — a demographic catastrophe that is one of the largest-scale pre-modern mass killings in mainland Southeast Asia. This separate entity frames Vijaya as a distinct long-term phase rather than a footnote to umbrella-Champa.

territory_changes.description: Cham elite shifted south to Vijaya (Đồ Bàn) after the destruction of Indrapura by Lê Hoàn (982). A new phase of Cham temple building began — the Bánh Ít, Cánh Tiên and Dương Long tower complexes.
Khmer king Suryavarman II invaded and briefly occupied Vijaya, installing a Khmer prince. The Cham recovered under Jaya Harivarman I (c. 1149).

Cham king Jaya Indravarman IV led a famous naval counter-invasion up the Mekong, sacking Angkor — an event memorialised in the bas-reliefs of the Bayon. This represents the high point of Cham military power.

Emperor Lê Thánh Tông of Đại Việt sacked Vijaya in a major campaign; Vietnamese chronicles record 60,000 Cham killed and 30,000 captured. The Cham polity was reduced to the southern rump of Panduranga.

sources.citation: Maspero, G. Le Royaume de Champa. Paris: Van Oest, 1928
Whitmore, J.K. 'The Development of Le Government in Fifteenth Century Vietnam', PhD dissertation, Cornell University, 1968
Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư, Ngô Sĩ Liên, 1479, entries for the 1471 campaign

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Panduranga is the southernmost region of Champa; as a distinct vassal or allied polity under the umbrella Cham mandala from the 8th century onward, and as the final Cham rump state after the 1471 destruction of Vijaya. The final abolition in 1832 by Emperor Minh Mạng ended the last Cham political structure after 1,640 years of Cham statehood — a continuity longer than most European national histories. The Cham community survived as a marginalized ethnic minority in Vietnam and Cambodia; contemporary Cham identity politics continues to draw on Panduranga's memory. This is the 'Cham national survival' entity.

name_variants.name: पाण्डुरंग
name_variants.context: Sanskrit: 'White Ash' / an epithet of Vishnu

name_variants.name: Thuận Thành
name_variants.context: formal Vietnamese protectorate name imposed after 1697

territory_changes.description: Panduranga is first attested as a distinct Cham regional mandala in the Virabhadra temple inscription; the southern Cham territory had its own prince and Shaiva religious tradition centred on Po Nagar and Po Klaung Girai.
After the destruction of Vijaya, Panduranga became the last independent Cham polity, reduced to about 1/3 of Champa's former area.
The Nguyễn lords made Panduranga a protectorate ('Thuận Thành') with a Cham sub-king, ending de facto independence.

Emperor Minh Mạng abolished Thuận Thành during the Lê Văn Khôi revolt, ending all Cham political structures. Forced assimilation, land confiscation, and the suppression of Cham Bani (indigenous Muslim) and Cham Jat (Hindu) practices followed. Large-scale Cham displacement to Cambodia produced the diaspora communities that still exist today.

sources.citation: Po Dharma. Le Panduranga (Campa) 1802-1835: Ses rapports avec le Vietnam, 2 vols. Paris: EFEO, 1987
Weber, N. 'The Destruction and Assimilation of Campa (1832) as seen from Cam Sources', Journal of Southeast Asian Studies 43.1 (2012), pp. 158-180

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Yenisei Kyrgyz Khaganate emerged when the Kyrgyz destroyed the Uyghur Khaganate in 840 CE, killing Qaghan Kürebir. For 80+ years they dominated the steppes from the Yenisei to the Tianshan, but never established the bureaucratic depth of their predecessors — 19th-c. historiography called this the 'Kyrgyz Grand Power'. The khaganate is ancestor to the modern Kyrgyz nation and to the Sary-Kyrgyz migration patterns of central Inner Asia. Its disproportionate impact on Inner Asian history — ending Uyghur dominance, scattering Uyghur refugees to create Qocho and Ganzhou — makes it central to the 9th-10th-c. steppe story.

territory_changes.description: The Kyrgyz under a khaganate led by a leader named in Chinese records as Are destroyed the Uyghur capital of Ordu-Baliq and killed the last Uyghur khagan Kürebir. Surviving Uyghurs fled south to found Qocho and Ganzhou.

The Khitan Liao under Yelü Abaoji expelled the Kyrgyz from Mongolia proper, pushing them back to their Yenisei homeland. The 'great power' phase ended; the Kyrgyz remained dominant in the Yenisei-Minusinsk region but no longer over the whole steppe.

sources.citation: Drompp, M.R. Tang China and the Collapse of the Uighur Empire: A Documentary History. Leiden: Brill, 2005
Golden, P.B. An Introduction to the History of the Turkic Peoples. Wiesbaden: Harrassowitz, 1992
Xin Tang Shu (新唐書, 'New Book of Tang'), compiled 1060 CE, chapter 217B on the Kyrgyz

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Qocho (also Idiqut Uyghuria or Uyghur Turpan Kingdom) was founded by Uyghur refugees after the Kyrgyz destruction of their 840 CE Mongolian khaganate. Centered on Turpan, it was a major Buddhist, Manichaean and later Nestorian Christian cultural centre, producing the enormously rich 'Turfan manuscripts' (now dispersed between Berlin, Kyoto, St. Petersburg and Beijing collections). Voluntarily submitted to the Mongols in 1209; their loyalty preserved the kingdom as a Mongol client through the 13th century. The gradual Islamicization from the 14th century onward and absorption into the Chagatai Khanate ended the Buddhist Uyghur kingdom — a major transformation of Inner Asian religious geography.

name_variants.name: Idiqut Uyghuria
name_variants.context: academic designation using the Uyghur royal title idikut / iduq qut

name_variants.name: Gaochang
name_variants.context: Chinese name of the capital and kingdom

name_variants.name: 西州回鹘
name_variants.context: Song-era Chinese designation: 'Uyghur of the Western Region'

territory_changes.description: Mengli, leader of a Uyghur refugee band, established himself in the Turfan region after decades of exile post-840; Tang sources recognise the kingdom by 866.

Iduq Qut Barchuq Art Tegin voluntarily submitted Qocho to Chinggis Khan, executing the Qara Khitai resident and marching west. Mongol policy favoured Uyghur literacy and administration; the Uyghur script was adopted for Mongolian writing.
Incorporation into the Chagatai Khanate; progressive Islamicization of the Uyghur population began, though Buddhist communities persisted for another century in some valleys.

sources.citation: Mackerras, C. 'The Uighurs', in Sinor, D. (ed.) The Cambridge History of Early Inner Asia. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1990
Allsen, T.T. Culture and Conquest in Mongol Eurasia. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2001
Moriyasu, T. 'Uighur Buddhism', in Bussei Nenshū (Annual of Buddhist Studies), 1991

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Ganzhou Uyghur Kingdom was the second successor state to the 840 collapse of the Uyghur Khaganate, in the Gansu corridor. Buddhist, Manichaean mixed; controlled a major segment of the Silk Road during the Five Dynasties and early Song period; annexed by the Tanguts (Western Xia) in 1036. This entity fills a major gap in AtlasPI coverage of the northwest Chinese border polities; it is historically and commercially as important as the Qocho kingdom of Turpan.

name_variants.name: Hexi Uyghur
name_variants.context: English alternative based on the Hexi corridor region

name_variants.name: Yellow-head Uyghur
name_variants.context: post-conquest label used from the Tangut period

territory_changes.description: A second group of Uyghur refugees from the 840 collapse consolidated at Ganzhou, exploiting the collapse of Tibetan control after the 842 assassination of Langdarma.

The Tangut king Li Yuanhao conquered Ganzhou and incorporated it into the expanding Western Xia state; surviving Uyghurs were dispersed or assimilated, surviving later as the Yugur (Yellow-head Uyghur) ethnic minority in modern Gansu.

sources.citation: Dunnell, R.W. The Great State of White and High: Buddhism and State Formation in Eleventh-Century Xia. Honolulu: University of Hawai'i Press, 1996
Pinks, E. Die Uiguren von Kan-chou in der frühen Sung-Zeit (960-1028). Wiesbaden: Harrassowitz, 1968

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Buayan is the pre-Islamic Iranun/Maguindanaon polity of the Cotabato basin that preceded the Maguindanao Sultanate (already in DB). Pre-Islamic Mindanao polities are under-documented in both Spanish colonial and Islamic post-conversion sources because both treated the pre-conversion period as unremarkable 'paganism'. The Buayan line persisted as a parallel Raja house to the Maguindanao Sultan line for several generations, highlighting that 'the' Maguindanao Sultanate was in fact a confederation. Confidence is low because documentation is late and post-Islamic.

name_variants.name: Sa Raya
name_variants.context: 'Upstream' polity, contrasting with Sa Ilud ('downstream', i.e. Maguindanao proper)

territory_changes.description: Approximate date of polity formation; the Buayan line emerged among Iranun- and Maguindanaon-speaking populations along the Rio Grande de Mindanao.
Islamic conversion under the influence of Sharif Kabungsuwan transformed Buayan into the upstream component of the Maguindanao Sultanate (founded 1515 in DB).

sources.citation: Majul, C.A. Muslims in the Philippines. Quezon City: University of the Philippines Press, 1973
Warren, J.F. The Sulu Zone 1768-1898: The Dynamics of External Trade, Slavery and Ethnicity in the Transformation of a Southeast Asian Maritime State. Singapore: NUS Press, 2nd ed. 2007

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Galuh is the Sundanese sister-polity of the Sunda Kingdom (Pajajaran, already in DB). Founded by Wretikandayun after the death of Tarumanagara's king Linggawarman, Galuh controlled the eastern Priangan highlands while Sunda controlled the western lowlands. The two repeatedly merged and split; final merger under Prabu Siliwangi in 1482 produced the unified Sunda-Galuh Pajajaran kingdom. West Javanese Sundanese history is often subsumed within Javanese (Mataram) historiography, flattening the real political separation.

territory_changes.description: After Linggawarman of Tarumanagara died, his son-in-law Tarusbawa inherited Sunda while Linggawarman's brother Wretikandayun claimed Galuh, formalised as an independent kingdom by 670 CE per the Carita Parahyangan chronicle.
Prabu Siliwangi (Jaya Dewata), already king of Galuh, was crowned king of Sunda as well, unifying both as the Sunda-Galuh kingdom known in later sources simply as Pajajaran.

sources.citation: Noorduyn, J. & Teeuw, A. Three Old Sundanese Poems. Leiden: KITLV Press, 2006
Carita Parahyangan, Old Sundanese chronicle, c. 16th c. palm-leaf manuscript

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Zhao Rugua's Zhu Fan Zhi (1225) and the later Daoyi Zhilüe list a series of small Sumatran polities between the fall of Srivijaya (Palembang) and the rise of Pasai/Malayu-Dharmasraya. 'Ta-hua-lo' (答花羅) is one of these, possibly at Jambi uplands. Low confidence because neither the exact identification nor the dates are settled; however, inclusion with explicit uncertainty captures the important 'interregnum' in Sumatran political geography that is otherwise invisible between the Srivijaya umbrella and the Pasai sultanate in the DB. If better scholarship clarifies, this entity should be corrected or merged.

name_variants.name: 答花羅
name_variants.context: Chinese transcription in Zhu Fan Zhi and Daoyi Zhilüe

name_variants.name: Tan-ma-ling (tentative)
name_variants.context: scholars have variously identified this polity with Tan-ma-ling on the peninsula or with Jambi uplands

territory_changes.description: Ta-hua-lo appears in Zhao Rugua's Zhu Fan Zhi among independent Sumatran ports following Srivijaya's fragmentation.
The polity is no longer distinguished in Chinese sources; possibly merged into Malayu-Dharmasraya or into the rising Majapahit-vassal Jambi polity.

sources.citation: Zhao Rugua, Zhu Fan Zhi (諸蕃志), 1225; trans. F. Hirth & W.W. Rockhill, St Petersburg, 1911
Wolters, O.W. 'Tāmbraliṅga', BSOAS 21 (1958), pp. 587-607

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Tambralinga is the Theravada Buddhist city-state of the Thai peninsular east coast, Srivijayan vassal for centuries then independent in the 12th-13th c. under King Chandrabhanu, who launched unsuccessful Ligor invasions of Sri Lanka in 1247 and 1262 — a rare case of Southeast Asian naval power projection into the Indian Ocean. Absorbed by Sukhothai and later Ayutthaya. Its 1247 Sri Lanka invasion is of considerable strategic-historical significance but rarely narrated.

name_variants.name: Ligor
name_variants.context: Portuguese and European designation of the capital Nakhon Si Thammarat

territory_changes.description: Approximate consolidation of the polity; early Indian-influenced Buddhist inscriptions begin to appear in the region by the 7th century.

King Chandrabhanu of Tambralinga invaded Sri Lanka with a force that briefly occupied parts of the Jaffna peninsula before being repelled by Parakramabahu II. A second expedition in 1262 ended with his death in battle.
The Nagarakretagama (1365) lists Dhārmmanagari (Tambralinga) as a Majapahit vassal; shortly after, Ayutthaya absorbed the polity directly.

sources.citation: Wolters, O.W. 'Tāmbraliṅga', Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies 21.3 (1958), pp. 587-607
Pathmanathan, S. 'The Kingdom of Jaffna: Origins and Early Development', Ceylon Journal of Historical and Social Studies, n.s. 5 (1975)
Cūḷavaṃsa, Chapter 83, Pali chronicle continuation of the Mahavamsa

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Gangga Negara is a Hindu-Buddhist Malay Peninsula kingdom in the Perak river basin attested in the Kedah Annals (Hikayat Merong Mahawangsa) and through scattered finds at Beruas (bronze Buddha of Gangga Negara, 10th c.). Destroyed by Rajendra Chola's 1025 raid alongside Kedah Tua and Langkasuka. The polity's visibility in Malay national historiography is shaped by the politically sensitive pre-Islamic legacy of the Malay Peninsula; including it with explicit uncertainty respects both the evidence and the historiographic caution.

territory_changes.description: Traditional founding date per the Kedah Annals; archaeology suggests a polity functioning from about the 3rd century CE onward.
Rajendra Chola's expedition destroyed the polity in the same campaign that sacked Kedah and Langkasuka. Surviving tradition is preserved only in Kedah oral history.

sources.citation: Wheatley, P. The Golden Khersonese. Kuala Lumpur: University of Malaya Press, 1961
Hikayat Merong Mahawangsa (Kedah Annals), 1810 manuscript tradition

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Pre-Islamic Pahang (not to be confused with the later Pahang Sultanate founded 1470) was a Hindu-Buddhist polity attested by Siamese chronicles from the 13th century and by the Nagarakretagama (1365) as a Majapahit vassal. Ming records (1378-1379) describe it sending tribute as 'Peng-heng'. Absorbed by Melaka's Sultan Muzaffar Shah in 1454 as a vassalage, then formalised as a sultanate under Mansur Shah's son Muhammad Shah in 1470.

territory_changes.description: Polity appears in Siamese chronicles as a tributary of the Thai kingdom of Ligor (Tambralinga); independent or semi-independent between Sukhothai, Majapahit and Srivijaya spheres.
Melaka's Tun Perak campaigned successfully against Pahang; the local ruler was killed, Melakan suzerainty imposed. His daughter became one of Mansur Shah's queens.

sources.citation: Linehan, W. A History of Pahang. Kuala Lumpur: MBRAS, 1936 (reprinted 1973)
Ming Shi (明史), chapter 325, entry on Peng-heng

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Martaban Kingdom (Ramanyadesa) is the brief Mon restoration polity of Lower Burma founded by Wareru (a Mon-Shan former tax collector of Sukhothai service) in 1287 after the Mongol pressure broke the Pagan dynasty. Martaban was the capital before the seat moved to Pegu in 1369, at which point the polity is usually renamed Hanthawaddy (Pegu, already in DB). This entity captures the first 82 years of the Mon restoration, distinct from the 1369+ Pegu phase. Historiography tends to collapse the two, losing political texture.

name_variants.name: Ramanyadesa
name_variants.context: Pali name meaning 'Land of the Mon', used in Mon chronicles

territory_changes.description: Wareru, a Mon-Shan noble formerly serving the Sukhothai court, declared independence at Martaban as the Pagan dynasty collapsed under Mongol pressure. He married a Sukhothai princess, anchoring the new polity between Tai and Mon political traditions.
King Binnya U moved the capital from Martaban to Pegu (Hanthawaddy), inaugurating the classical Hanthawaddy phase already recorded in the AtlasPI database as ဟံသာဝတီ.

sources.citation: Aung-Thwin, M. The Mists of Ramañña. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 2005
Lieberman, V. Strange Parallels: Southeast Asia in Global Context, c. 800-1830, Vol. 1: Integration on the Mainland. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2003

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Samal Balangingi of the outer Sulu archipelago were a distinct polity allied to but often operationally independent of the Sulu Sultanate (already in DB). Their seaborne raiding, vilified as 'piracy' in Spanish colonial sources, was better understood as a tributary-slaving state economy (Warren, Sulu Zone). Spanish forces under Narciso Claveria destroyed Balanguingui in 1848 with extraordinary brutality — a genocidal episode included here explicitly to resist its invisibility. The majority of surviving Balangingi were forcibly relocated as agricultural labourers in Luzon.

territory_changes.description: Spanish forces under Governor-General Narciso Claveria destroyed the Balanguingui fortifications in one of the most violent colonial operations in Philippine history. Survivors were deported to Luzon; the polity was liquidated.

sources.citation: Warren, J.F. The Sulu Zone 1768-1898, 2nd ed. Singapore: NUS Press, 2007
Warren, J.F. Iranun and Balangingi: Globalization, Maritime Raiding and the Birth of Ethnicity. Singapore: Singapore University Press, 2002

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The DB already contains 'Kerajaan Gelgel' (in batch_22) from the post-1550 period. This entity covers the earlier 1343-1550 phase under the Majapahit-aligned Kresna Kepakisan dynasty, before Batu Renggong's reign made Gelgel the paramount Balinese power. The phase is important because it cements the 'Majapahit refugee' trope in later Balinese historiography while also preserving Bedulu-era Shaiva institutions. Separation is warranted given the centuries-long distinct character.

name_variants.name: Kepakisan dynasty Gelgel
name_variants.context: academic designation distinguishing from later classical Gelgel

name_variants.name: Samprangan-Gelgel period
name_variants.context: alternative that notes the earlier capital at Samprangan

territory_changes.description: Gajah Mada of Majapahit installed Dalem Sri Kresna Kepakisan as regent-king at Samprangan; later moved to Gelgel. Majapahit suzerainty was more ritual than administrative.

Dalem Baturenggong's consolidation opened the classical Gelgel kingdom phase (already a DB entity). The polity underwent effectively a civilizational re-founding with the arrival of Dang Hyang Nirartha and his religious reforms.

sources.citation: Hägerdal, H. Hindu Rulers, Muslim Subjects: Lombok and Bali in the Seventeenth and Eighteenth Centuries. Bangkok: White Lotus Press, 2001
Vickers, A. Bali: A Paradise Created. Hong Kong: Periplus, 1989

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Salakanagara is the semi-legendary Sundanese 'Silver City' kingdom placed in west Java from 130 to 362 CE, recorded only in the late Naskah Wangsakerta — a problematic 17th-century manuscript compilation whose authenticity is disputed (many scholars consider it a late invention). Confidence is deliberately very low and status 'disputed' to avoid laundering the Wangsakerta's questionable content into the database as fact. Including the entity with explicit uncertainty serves pedagogy: the Wangsakerta debate itself is a methodological lesson about Indonesian historiography. If the Wangsakerta is rehabilitated (unlikely), the entity stands; if definitively discredited, it should be deleted.

name_variants.name: Argyre
name_variants.context: Ptolemy's 'Argyre' / 'Silver Island' has been speculatively identified with Salakanagara; the identification is extremely tentative

territory_changes.description: Founding by Dewawarman per the Naskah Wangsakerta; no contemporary epigraphic or archaeological evidence confirms the date or the polity.
Succeeded by Tarumanagara (already in DB) per the Wangsakerta; the transition is entirely narrative and unverifiable.

sources.citation: Naskah Wangsakerta, 17th-c. Cirebon manuscript tradition (authenticity disputed)
Lombard, D. Le carrefour javanais: essai d'histoire globale. Paris: EHESS, 1990 (sceptical commentary on Wangsakerta)
